title: Hard-dough bread
subtitle: Jamaican enriched white bread
excerpt: A slightly dense, slightly sweet white bread with a tight, chewy crumb and a thin, glossy crust. Hard-dough bread is less open than a standard white sandwich loaf and more firm — the name reflects the tighter dough used to make it. It is the bread of the Jamaican breakfast table, served alongside ackee and saltfish, used for escovitch fish, and sold from every bakery in Jamaica.
sourceNotes: Hard-dough bread is a Jamaican staple with roots in the colonial-era bakeries that adapted British bread-baking traditions. The characteristic dense, chewy crumb comes from a lower hydration dough than standard white bread. It is sold in Jamaican bakeries across the UK and is available in some supermarkets. The home-baked version requires no special equipment beyond a loaf tin.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice before freezing and toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be shaped and cold-proved overnight in the fridge. Remove 30 minutes before baking.

content.text: Hard-dough bread uses less water than a standard white loaf — around 58 to 60% hydration versus 65 to 70% for a standard sandwich loaf. The lower hydration is what gives it the dense, slightly chewy crumb. Do not be tempted to add more water if the dough feels stiff; that stiffness is correct.

The bread is traditionally glazed with an egg wash before baking for a shiny crust. A light brushing of water before baking also works if you prefer to avoid egg. The crust should be pale golden, not dark — this is not a rustic loaf.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{yeast-dried}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Add the {{vegetable-oil}}, {{whole-milk}}, and 120 ml of warm water. Mix to a firm dough — it should be slightly stiffer than a standard bread dough. Knead for 10 minutes on a lightly floured surface until smooth and elastic. The dough will not feel as soft as a sandwich loaf dough; this is correct. Place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and leave in a warm place to rise for 1 hour until doubled.

Knock back the dough and shape it into a smooth log that fits a lightly oiled 900 g loaf tin. Place seam-side down in the tin, cover, and prove for 45 minutes to 1 hour until it has risen to just above the tin rim. Heat the oven to 190°C. Brush the top with beaten {{eggs}} or water. Bake for 32 to 35 minutes until deep golden on top and the loaf sounds hollow when tapped on the base. Cool on a wire rack for at least 20 minutes before slicing.

items.symptom: Bread too dense and barely risen
items.cause: Yeast inactive or dough too cold
items.fix: Check the yeast is fresh. Prove in a warm place (25–28°C). A cold kitchen will slow the rise significantly
Hard-dough bread should be golden, not dark brown. Reduce the temperature by 10°C and check at 30 minutes

items.cause: Too much water added to the dough
items.fix: The characteristic dense crumb comes from a stiff dough. Add only as much liquid as specified

content.text: Hard-dough bread is the bread of Jamaica. It is sold from every bakery on the island and has been the household bread for generations. In Britain it is found in Caribbean bakeries and some supermarkets — a white sliced loaf shaped and textured differently from standard British bread, denser and more satisfying as the base for a plate of ackee and saltfish. The home-baked version is rarely attempted in the UK diaspora because the commercial product is widely available, but the homemade loaf is noticeably better.
